coredata.dc:title: Functionalized layered double hydroxide with nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated carbondots for highly selective and efficient removal of soft Hg2+ and Ag+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Asiabi, Hamid
Yamini, Yadollah
Shamsayei, Maryam
Molaei, Karam
Shamsipur, Mojtaba
coredata.dc:description: A facile composite was fabricated via direct assembly of nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated carbon dots with abundant oxygen–containing functional groups on the surface of the positively charged layered double hydroxide (N,S-CDs-LDH). The novel N,S-CDs-LDH demonstrates highly selective bindings (M-S) and an extremely efficient removal capacity for soft metal ions such as Ag+ and Hg2+ ions. N,S-CDs-LDH displayed a selectivity order of Ag+> Hg2+ >> Cu2+ >>> Pb2+ > Zn2+ > Cd2+ for their adsorption. The enormous capacities for Hg2+ (625.0 mg g−1) and Ag+ (714.3 mg g−1) and very high distribution coefficients (Kd ) of 9.9 × 106 mL g−1 (C0 = 20 mg L−1) and 2.0 × 107 mL g−1 (C0 = 20 mg L−1) for Hg2+ and Ag+, respectively, place the N,S-CDs-LDH at the top of LDH based materials known for such removal. The adsorption kinetic curves for Hg2+ and Ag+ fitted well with the pseudo-second order model. For Hg2+ and Ag+, an exceptionally rapid capture with removal ∼100% within 80 min was observed (Cions = 30 mg L−1 and V/m ratio of 1000). The adsorption isotherms were well described using Langmuir isotherm. The N,S-CDs-LDH was successfully applied to highly efficient removal of Hg2+ and Ag+ from aqueous solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Heavy metal ions
Layered double hydroxides
Removal
Selective

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Relative selectivity for Cu2+, Ag+ and Hg2+
Hg2+ and Ag+ removal as a function of pH
Adsorption isotherms towards Hg2+ and Ag+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Tarbiat Modares University References . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic proposed view of Hg2+ adsorption on N,S-CDs-LDH.

(a) TEM and (b) AFM images of the N, S-CDs. (c) Size distribution of the N, S-CDs. (d) height profile along the line 1. (e) FTIR spectrum of the N,S-CDs. (f) The UV–vis absorption, PL excitation and emission spectra of the aqueous dispersion of N,S-CDs. Inset: image of the N, S-CDs dispersion under UV light (365 nm).

(a) XRD patterns of LDH, N,S-CDs and N,S-CDs-LDH, (b) FT-IR spectra of LDH and N,S-CDs-LDH, (c) TEM image of LDH, (d) TEM image of N,S-CDs-LDH, (e) SEM image of N,S-CDs-LDH and (f) EDS mapping analysis of N,S-CDs-LDH.
Fitting plots of (a) Langmuir and (b) Freundlich isotherm models for Hg2+ and Ag+ adsorption onto N,S-CDs-LDH.
(a) Pseudo-second order kinetic plot and (b) Pseudo-first order kinetic plot for the adsorption of Hg2+ and Ag+ on N,S-CDs-LDH.
Adsorption capacity of N,S-Cds-LDH toward individual metal ions.
Adsorption results of N,S-Cds-LDH toward individual metal ions.
Adsorption of N,S-Cds-LDH toward the mixed ions.
Adsorption of N,S-Cds-LDH toward the mixed ions.
Parameters of Langmuir and Freundlich equations for adsorption of Hg and Ag ions on N,S-Cds–LDH.
Comparison of qm values of some sorbents reported in the literature for the removal of target metal ions.
Calculated parameters of the pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order kinetics models for adsorption of Hg and Ag onto N,S-Cds-LDH.

head.ce:title: Functionalized layered double hydroxide with nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated carbondots for highly selective and efficient removal of soft Hg2+ and Ag+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, P.O. Box 14115-175, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile composite was fabricated via direct assembly of nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated carbon dots with abundant oxygen–containing functional groups on the surface of the positively charged layered double hydroxide (N,S-CDs-LDH). The novel N,S-CDs-LDH demonstrates highly selective bindings (M-S) and an extremely efficient removal capacity for soft metal ions such as Ag+ and Hg2+ ions. N,S-CDs-LDH displayed a selectivity order of Ag+> Hg2+ >> Cu2+ >>> Pb2+ > Zn2+ > Cd2+ for their adsorption. The enormous capacities for Hg2+ (625.0 mg g−1) and Ag+ (714.3 mg g−1) and very high distribution coefficients (Kd ) of 9.9 × 106 mL g−1 (C0 = 20 mg L−1) and 2.0 × 107 mL g−1 (C0 = 20 mg L−1) for Hg2+ and Ag+, respectively, place the N,S-CDs-LDH at the top of LDH based materials known for such removal. The adsorption kinetic curves for Hg2+ and Ag+ fitted well with the pseudo-second order model. For Hg2+ and Ag+, an exceptionally rapid capture with removal ∼100% within 80 min was observed (Cions = 30 mg L−1 and V/m ratio of 1000). The adsorption isotherms were well described using Langmuir isotherm. The N,S-CDs-LDH was successfully applied to highly efficient removal of Hg2+ and Ag+ from aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: In the last decades, the removal of toxic heavy-metal ions such as Hg2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Zn2+ and Cu2+ from wastewater resources has received significant attention because of their hazards to the environment and living organisms [1,2]. For example, mercury is a notorious heavy metal which produces harmful effects on the nervous, digestive and immune systems, lungs and kidneys, and thus poses a severe risk for human beings and other organisms [3,4]. The Word Health Organization (WHO) has announced a maximum acceptable Hg2+ concentration of 1 μg L−1 in drinking water [5]. Silver particles are a precious metal widely used in the photography, catalysis, electronics, chemical, medication and jewelery industries [6–8]. However, silver ions in drinking water can be potentially hazardous to health [6,9,10]. The WHO has set the maximum Ag+ concentration in drinking water to 0.1 mg L−1 [6]. Therefore, the removal of heavy metal ions from wastewater has become a crucial issue. Recently, various physical–chemical processes have been extensively used in the treatment of heavy metal ions in wastewater [11–13]. Among those methods, the use of adsorbents is considered as an efficient technique for removing toxic metal ions from the aqueous medium because of their numerous advantages such as high removal efficiency, easy operation, green structure and low cost [14,15].

In recent years, clay minerals have generagted increasing interest as adsorbents because of their high surface area, hydrophilic character, high adsorption sites and low cost [16,17]. Among various clay minerals, layered double hydroxides (LDHs) have aroused much interest in the field of removal of heavy metal ions due to their layer structure, simple synthesis, low cost and environmental friendliness [18,19]. The structural formula of LDHs can be expressed as [M2+ 1−xM3+ x (OH)2]x+(An−)x/n·mH2O, where M2+ , M3+ and An− represent divalent metal cations, trivalent metal cations and inter-layer anions, respectively [20,21]. Recently, composite materials prepared by the intercalation of well-known compounds such as amino tris (methylene phosphonic acid) [22], chromotropic acid [23] and etilendiaminetetracetate [24] into LDHs have been reported to be able to adsorb metal cations, suggesting that LDH-based materials are promising adsorbents for metal cations. However, these materials suffer from low selectivity and weak affinity for heavy metal ions. In any case, the selectivity and adsorption efficiency of LDHs for specific metal ions can be improved by intercalating suitable inter-layer anions [14,25–27].

Thiol-functionalized materials show remarkable selectivity and binding affinity for a variety of soft heavy metal ions [28–30]. Recently, polysulfide- and MoS4-intercalated LDHs were reported for selective and efficient removal of soft metal ions. But synthesis process of polysulfide is very difficult and dangerous [13,14]. Also, chemical stability of polysulfide under the ambient conditions is very low. For these reasons, polysulfide isn’t a good option for improving adsorption properties of LDHs. Also, MoS4-intercalated LDH suffers from low adsorption capacity for Hg2+ (500 mg g−1) compared to the polysulfide-LDHs (686 mg g−1). The development selectivity factor and adsorption capacity of LDHs is still in its infancy. In this work two goals are followed: (1) synthesis LDHs with high selectivity factor and adsorption capacity for soft metal ions and (2) use of completely green materials with easy synthesis procedure for modification of LDHs.

Carbon nano-dots (CDs) have also received considerable attention due to their extraordinary properties such as lower cost, robust chemical inertness, good biocompatibility, easy functionalization and low toxicity [31–33]. The CDs can be grafted on the surface of the positively charged LDHs with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups of -OH and -COOH on the surface of CDs to generate composites with good stability and high adsorption capacity. Also, thiol functionalized CDs seem to be effective for heavy metal remediation, as the high affinity of soft Lewis basic frameworks for the soft Lewis acids (e.g. Hg2+, Ag+ and Cu2+) is innate to these materials [27].

Herein, sodium citrate and L-cysteine were used to produce nitrogen and sulfur co-decorated CDs (N,S-CDs) through a one-step hydrothermal treatment. Then, we report a synthesis of CDs-LDH composites via simple co-precipitation LDH formation method in the presence of N,S-CDs. Specially, in -NH2 and -SH functionalized-CDs, the higher capacity of soft heavy metal ion adsorption arises due to the open sulfide framework structure, soft Lewis base nature of sulfide anions which has higher affinity towards soft Lewis acidic heavy metal ion center, and strong heavy metal complexing capability of amino groups. We show that N,S-CDs-LDHs are suitable materials for the removal of Hg2+ and Ag+ ions, which is consistent with the hypothesis that Hg2+⋯ S2− and Ag+⋯ S2−bonding interactions can selectively remove these ions. The as-prepared N,S-CDs-LDHs sorbent exhibits enormous selectivity, excellent removal capacity and also fast trapping of Ag+ and Hg2+ ions.

All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. Mg(NO3)2.6H2O, Al(NO3)3.9H2O, Cd(NO3)2.4H2O, Cu(NO3)2.3H2O, Zn(NO3)2.6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Hg(NO3)2.H2O, Ag(NO3) salts were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI, USA). L-cysteine and sodium citrate were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Other chemicals applied were of analytical reagent grade or of the highest purity available. Apparatus information is completely explained in the supporting information section.

The N,S-CDs were synthesized by the thermal treatment of molecular organic salts with the mixed carbon source and the surface modifier in the single precursor [34]. In a typical synthesis, 300 mg of sodium citrate and 100 mg of L-cysteine were added to 30 mL of distilled water. After vigorous stirring, the transparent solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and then heated at 160 °C for 10 h. The autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The resultant yellow solution was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles and then dialyzed with ultrapure water using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff 3500 Da for 24 h to remove unreacted primary materials and inorganic salts. Finally, the purified N,S-CDs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

N,S-CDs-LDH composites were directly synthesized using separate nucleation and aging steps [35]. Briefly, 150 mL of a CO2-free aqueous solution containing 0.02 mol Mg(NO3)2.6H2O and 0.01 mol Al(NO3)2.9H2O was prepared and added dropwise into a 100 mL N,S-CDs colloidal solution under vigorous stirring conditions. During the addition of the metal ions solutions, the pH of the reaction mixture was adjusted to 9.5–10.0 by adding 2 M of the NaOH solution. The resulting gelatinous precipitate was aged at 80 °C for 24 h. The final product was collected by centrifuge (4000 rpm for 5 min), washed with water and ethanol five times, and dried at 70 °C for 6 h.

The heavy metal removal from aqueous solutions with various concentrations were studied using the batch method. Typically, 10 mg of the adsorbent was added into 20 mL of the solution containing a certain amount of the six ions of Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+. The mixture was stirred for 3 h at a constant temperature of 25 °C. After mixing the solid sorbents with the solutions for a certain time, centrifugation was performed and the metal concentrations in the supernatant solutions were determined using inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) for extra low ion concentration. ICP-AES operating conditions and metal ions emission lines were shown in Table S1.
The adsorptive capacity was evaluated based on the difference of metal concentrations in mother and supernatant solutions.

The removal efficiency, the amount of metal ions adsorbed qt (mg g−1) and the distribution coefficient (Kd ) were calculated according to Eqs. (1)–(3), respectively:
(1)
% R e m o v a l =

C o

C t
)
C o
× 100
(2)
q t
=
C o

C t
V
m
(3)
K d
=
(
C o

C t
) V
C t
× m
×
10 3
where C0 (mg L−1) is the initial concentration of metal ions, Ct (mg L−1) is the metal ions concentration at time t (min), m (g) is the mass of the adsorbent, V (L) is the volume of the adsorbate solution and qt (mg g−1) is the adsorbed amount at time t (min).

To determine the selectivity of the selected sorbent to metal ions, some experiments containing them all together were carried out. Then, for distinguishing the high selective ions of Cu2+, Hg2+ and Ag+, an experiment containing only Cu2+, Hg2+ and Ag+ in the solution was carried out. A concentration of 10 mg L−1 of each ion mixed with a small quantity of N,S-CDs-LDH (20, 10 and 5 mg), which was adequate to pick up one of the metal ions, was used. The separation factor for A and B (SFA B) was used to indicate the selectivity of the N,S-CDs-LDH for the metal ions. The SFA B defined as Kd A/Kd B is a scale to determine whether a material can be used to separate out certain ions from one another [14].
Schematic proposed view of Hg2+ adsorption on N,S-CDs-LDH was shown in Fig. 1 .

The adsorption isotherms for metal ions were established as follows: 10 mg of N,S-CDs-LDH was added to 20 mL of the aqueous solution containing a certain ion with concentrations varying from 30 to 1000 mg L−1 and stirred for 4 h at a constant temperature of 25 °C. Then, centrifugation was performed and the metal concentrations in the supernatant solutions were determined. Adsorption isotherms can illustrate the maximum adsorption capacity of an adsorbent for metal ions. Therefore, the adsorption isotherm data of Hg2+ and Ag+ were fitted to the Langmuir (Eq. (4)) and Freundlich (Eq. (5)) equations as shown below [36,37]:
(4)
q e
=
q m
b
C e
1 + b
C e
(5)
q e
=
K . C
e
1 / n
Where Ce (mg L−1) and qe (mg g−1) are the equilibrium adsorbate concentrations in the aqueous and solid phases, respectively, qm (mg g−1) is the maximum adsorption capacity, and b is the Langmuir adsorption equilibrium constant. The parameters k and 1/n are related to the Freundlich equilibrium constant.

Sorption kinetics was investigated to explain the sorption mechanism and characteristics of Hg2+ and Ag+ ions on N,S-CDs-LDH. Adsorption kinetic experiments for the ions of Ag+ and Hg2+ under various adsorption times (1–240 min) were performed. For each operation, 20 mg of the sorbent was added to 20 mL of the aqueous solution containing a certain ion (20 mg L−1). At specified time intervals, the suspensions were centrifuged and 2 mL of the supernatant solutions were taken and analyzed by ICP-AES. In order to maintain the V/m ratio constant, to determine the adsorbed amount at any times, the fresh sample solution was used. Pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order kinetics models were applied to fit the experimental data and its equation y, which are expressed as follows:
(6)
F i r s t - o r d e r
e q u a t i o n :
log
q e
-
q t
=
log
q e
-
K 1
2.303
t
(7)
S e c o n d - o r d e r
e q u a t i o n :
t
q t
=
1
(
K 2
q e 2
)
+
1
q e
t
Where qe and qt are the amounts of metal ions adsorbed at equilibrium and at time t (mg g−1), respectively. k1 (min−1) and k2 (g mg−1 min−1) are in sequence the rate constants of first-order and second-order adsorption.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) were applied to examine the morphology and particle size of the as-prepared N,S-CDs. The TEM image (Fig. 2 a), AFM image (Fig. 2b) and histogram (Fig. 2c) of the N,S-CDs show that the size of the prepared N,S-CDs is distributed in the range from 4 to 10 nm. Based on Fig. 2d, the height profile shows that the heights of the N,S-CDs are in the range of 0.6–1.2 nm, confirming the single-layer feature. EDS analysis proved the uniform distribution of NH2 and SH inside N,S-CDs (Fig. S1).

FT-IR spectroscopy was carried out to investigate the chemical composition and functional groups of the N,S-CDs. FT-IR spectrum of the N,S-CDs (Fig. 2e) displays characteristic peaks at 1405, 2051, 1226 and 2954 cm−1 corresponding to CC, CN, CN and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. The peak at 1620 cm−1 can be assigned to an amide carbonyl-stretching mode. Clearly, the carbonyl band in N,S-CDs is much stronger than those of sodium citrate and cysteine, implying that the N,S-CDs include more carbonyl bands. The peak at 3421 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. The absorption band at 678 cm−1 could be assigned to the CS groups. Based on the results, the nitrogen and sulfur atoms are successfully doped to the CDs.

The optical properties of the N,S-CDs were investigated using the UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 2f). It can be seen that there is a broad absorption peak centered around 350 nm (black line) in the UV–vis spectrum which can be ascribed to the n-π* transitions of CO bands [38]. A strong fluorescence emission spectrum centered at 444 nm (blue line) is observed when it is excited at 350 nm (red line). The strong fluorescence of the N,S-CDs may originate from the emissive traps of the nitrogen and the sulfur-doped surface. A series of emission spectra of the N,S-CDs were carried out at various excitation wavelengths in the range of 300–380 nm to study the optical properties of the N,S-CDs in more detail. The excitation wavelength independent property of the N,S-CDs can be seen in Fig. S2. The photograph of the aqueous dispersion of N,S-CDs displays a blue color under UV light illumination (Fig. 2d), further revealing that the resultant N,S-CDs emit blue fluorescence.

The XRD pattern obtained for the LDH exhibited sharp and symmetric (003), (006), (012), (015), (018), (110) and (113) reflections (Fig. 3 a) [22,23,39]. A (003) diffraction peak of a LDH structure is related to the brucite-like layer stacking and the interlayer distance. For the LDH, the observed basal spacing, d (003), was 11.67 Å due to the LDH host layer thickness of approximately 4.8 Å and the inter-layer spacing of 6.87 Å. The XRD pattern for the N,S-CDs shows a diffraction peak in the range of 17.46°–27.58° which corresponds to a graphitic structure [40,41]. The peak is broad due to the small size of the N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3a, the XRD pattern of the resulting N,S-CDs -LDH exhibits the superimposition of reflections of a N,S-CDs phase and a LDH phase. The FTIR spectra of LDH and N,S-CDs-LDH are shown in Fig. 3b. Like most reported LDHs, FTIR spectrum of the LDH confirmed the formation of the LDHs [22,23,39]. In comparison, the FTIR spectrum of N,S-CDs-LDH showed that there appeared a new adsorption band. FTIR spectra further confirmed the presence of oxygen-containing groups (OH, COO−, CO) and CS, CC, and CN groups [34]. These observations indicate that the N,S-CDs had been successfully loaded on the LDH.

The structure, size, and morphology of the fabricated hybrid were characterized by SEM and TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, the TEM image of the as-prepared LDH showed a platelet structure. In addition, the TEM image of N,S-CDs-LDH nanocomposites indicated that N,S-CDs were well dispersed on the surface of LDHs (Fig. 3d). From the SEM image (Fig. 3e), it can also be clearly seen that N,S-CDs-LDH consists of nanosheets with an average length of about 50–150 nm and a thickness of approximately 10 nm. In addition, the results of SEM-EDX analysis proved the uniform distribution of each element inside N,S-CDs-LDH (Fig. 3f).
Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis showed that the surface area of the N,S-CDs-LDH is 321.43 m2 g−1 which is much larger than those of NO3-LDH with a 62.75 m2 g−1 surface area.

The adsorption of Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Pb2+, Cd2+ and Hg2+ by N,S-CDs-LDH from the solution was studied by the batch method at room temperature. The affinity of N,S-CDs-LDH toward these ions can be expressed in terms of the adsorption capacity (qt ) and the distribution coefficient (Kd ). Adsorption experiments with individual solutions of metal ions were carried out. Two sets of metal ion concentrations were used: low values of 10 mg L−1 for assessing the Kd values, and higher values of 1000 mg L−1 for assessing qt values. Table 1 summarizes the qt values for removal of individual metal ions by N,S-CDs-LDH. As shown in Table 1, N,S-CDs-LDH provides a higher adsorption capacity for Hg2+ (qt = 572.0 mg g-1), Ag+ (qt = 625.4 mg g-1) and Cu2+ (qt = 526.2 mg g-1) than for the other metal ions. The observed order of qt of N,S-CDs-LDH for metal ions was Ag+>Hg2+>Cu2+>Pb2+ >>> Zn2+ > Cd2+. The affinity of the sorbent for metal ions can be expressed in terms of the Kd . In general, Kd values above 104 mL g−1 are considered to be exceptional sorbents [42]. The obtained Kd values of N,S-CDs-LDH toward individual metal ions are given in Table 2 . From this table, the general selectivity order for the six ions is as follows: Ag+>Hg2+>Cu2+>>Pb2+ >>> Zn2+, Cd2+. The results show that the adsorption ability and selectivity toward Hg2+, Ag+ and Cu2+ is much higher than all other ions. As shown in Table 2, the concentrations of Hg2+ and Ag+ decreased from the starting value of 10 mg L−1 to below 0.1 μg L−1, with nearly 100% removal achieved, well below the acceptable level in drinking water [5]. Based on these results, N,S-CDs-LDHs are effective in the selective adsorption of Hg2+ and Ag+ from their solutions. In fact, it can be said that the strong tendency of S atoms to bind with soft heavy metal ions acts as a strong driving force for the efficient removal of Hg2+ and Ag+.

Adsorption of the metal ions from 20 mL of a solution containing a mixture of all of the six ions with the initial concentrations of 10 mg L−1 was performed using 10 mg of N,S-CDs-LDH and the obtained results are shown in Table 3 . As shown in Table 3, the selectivity order of Ag+, Hg2+>Cu2+>>>Pb2+ > Zn2+>Cd2+ was observed, which is generally in agreement with those seen in single adsorption experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relative selectivity for Cu2+, Ag+ and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: From Table 3, it can also be seen that the N,S-CDs-LDH can rapidly reduce the concentrations of Cu2+, Hg2+ and Ag+ to a very low level. This makes it difficult to carry out experiments to assess the potential to separate these ions from one another when all of them are present in the solution. Moreover, it is interesting to see if N,S-CDs-LDH has any preference among these three ions, which might become the basis for separating them. For this purpose, the adsorption of a mixture of all three ions was performed using N,S-CDs-LDH. As shown in Table 3, Ag+ and Hg2+ have similar Kd values and they cannot be selectively separated using the N,S-CDs-LDH under the employed operating conditions. Thus, in order to distinguish the Ag+ from Hg2+ as well as Cu2+, the adsorption of a 20 mL solution containing a mixture of all three ions at a concentration of 10 mg L−1 was performed using decreased quantities (20.0 mg, 10.0 mg and 5.0 mg) of N,S-CDs-LDH and the obtained results are shown in Table 4 . According to the obtained Kd values, when using 20 mg of the sorbent, the values of SFHg Cu, SFAg Cu and SFAg Hg can be calculated as 7.62 × 103, 1.26 × 103, and 6.02 respectively, showing an obvious higher affinity and selectivity of N,S-CDs-LDH for Ag+ and Hg2+ than Cu2+. In other words, the selectivity order of Ag+, Hg2+>Cu2+ was observed using 20 mg of the adsorbent. Also, by using 10 mg, a clear order of Ag+>Hg2+>>Cu2+ emerges, and 5.0 mg shows an order of Ag+>>Hg2+>>>Cu2+ (Table 4). It can be observed that the efficiency of selective removal of heavy metal ions increased when the N,S-CDs-LDH dosage decreased. Remarkably, the maximum SFHg Cu, SFAg Cu and SFAg Hg of 2.29 × 102, 3.77 × 104 and 1.64 × 102 were obtained, which shows these values are very high compared to other reported adsorbents.

In the mining operations of precious metals, the ability to distinguish Ag+ and Hg2+ from one another is important [43]. Based on these results, N,S-CDs-LDH is highly effective in selectively removing Hg2+ and Ag+ commonly found in the mining of precious metals with an SFAg Hg of 1.64 × 102.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hg2+ and Ag+ removal as a function of pH
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical stability and adsorption capacity of N,S-CDs-LDH was comparatively tested at different pHs of 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10. N,S-CDs-LDH exhibits remarkable Hg2+ and Ag+ ion adsorption capacity with more than 99% of the ions adsorbed between pH 2–10 and the crystallinity of N,S-CDs-LDH was also maintained throughout all the experiments. However, for the sample loaded at pH < 2, we observed less adsorption capacity. This behavior is in accordance with the adsorption of Hg(II) observed in other sulfur-functionalized carbon materials at low pH, in which the positively charged carbon surface and also the positively charged mercury complex (like S–Hg+ or Hg2+) may act as an electrostatic barrier and prohibit further adsorption [44,45]. Also, the decrease in the adsorption capacity at low pHs (pH < 2.0) may be related to the slight dissolution of LDHs. Based on the ICP-AES results, the released amount of Mg and Al at pH < 2.0 reached to 0.34 and 0.21 mmol g−1, respectively. But at pHs above 2.0, concentrations of Mg and Al in solution are zero, that suggests N,S-CDs-LDHs are stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption isotherms towards Hg2+ and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Two equilibrium Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms were applied to describe the adsorption capacity of N,S-CDs-LDH towards Hg2+ and Ag+ ions. The isotherms of Hg2+ and Ag+ sorption on N,S-CDs-LDH are shown in Fig. S3. They were typical H type isotherms according to Giles classification [46] and type I isotherms based on classification of IUPAC [47]. This indicates that the adsorption of metal ions on N,S-CDs-LDH occurs as monolayer and illustrate very high affinity between the adsorbent and adsorbate. The fitting plots of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherm models for Hg2+ and Ag+ adsorption are shown in Fig. 4 . The results inserted in Table 5 show that the best fit of Hg2+ and Ag+ sorption was found by Langmuir model with a R2 >0.9974. The Langmuir isotherm is adsorption of the analytes in identical sites which are energetically uniform and there is a formation of monolayer coverage on the adsorbent surface after adsorbing metal ions. The adsorption capacity of the N,S-CDs-LDH material for Hg2+ and Ag+ ions was checked in the concentration range of 30 mg L-1 to 1000 mg L-1 (Tables S2 and S3). From the Langmuir equation fitting data, the maximum absorption capacities (qm ) of N,S-CDs-LDH for Hg2+ and Ag+ were 625.0 mg g−1 and 714.3 mg g−1 respectively. Comparing the qm values in Table 6 shows that the obtained qm values for N,S-CDs-LDH are comparable to or higher than those found for previously reported sorbents such as polysulfide-LDH [14], Layered K2MgSn2S6 [40], Zr-MOF [48], Thiol modified Fe3O4@SiO2 [49], magnetic cellulose xanthate [8], SH@SiO2/metal-organic framework [50], mercapto-functionalized mesoporous crystalline material-41 [51], PAF-1-SH [52], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [53,54].

The removl of heavy metal ions by NO3-LDH was studied by the batch method at room temperature and obtained results were inserted in Tables S4 and S5. Comparing the qm and Kd values of NO3-LDH and N,S-CDs-LDH show that the obtained qm and Kd values for N,S-CDs-LDH are higher than those found for NO3-LDH. Also obtained results at Table S5 show that NO3-LDH don’t show any selectivity to heavy metal ions.

XRD patterns of Hg2+ adsorbed N,S-CDs-LDH compared with the original N,S-CDs-LDH are shown in Fig. S4. It can be clearly observed that a series of LDH and CDs peaks of Hg-N,S-CDs-LDH samples appear to have had almost no change, which has also been previously found with regard to heavy metals sorption on intercalated layered double hydroxides adsorbents [54].

The adsorption of Hg2+and stability of N,S-CDs-LDH were also confirmed by the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S5). A band at 1384 cm−1 occured for all solid samples after the adsorption, implying the presence of NO3- anions,which accompanied the metal ion entrance into the adsobent for charge balance [14,55]. This indicates that the binding of metal ions to the N,S-CDs converts the N,S-CDs-LDH back to NO3–LDH plus the associated MSx species as a second phase [56]. Additionally, the unchanged band at 400 to 800 cm−1 assigned to MO, MOM, and OMO lattic vibrations indicates the total stability of the LDH layer after the adsorption.

The results suggest that the M-S complexes are accommodated on the sorbent without apparent deformations, dissolution or structure change of N,S-CDs-LDH. In addition, the remarkable structural stability of N,S-CDs-LDH enable them to exhibit a powerful ability to remove soft heavy metal cations in aqueous solutions.

To investigate the adsorption kinetics of Hg2+ and Ag+ onto N,S-CDs-LDH and to explain the sorption mechanism, characteristics and the rate-controlling step in the overall adsorption process, pseudo-first order and pseudo-second order kinetics models were applied to fit the experimental data. The Hg2+ and Ag+ sorption ratio onto N,S-CDs-LDH as a function of contact time is shown in Fig. S6 and listed in Tables S6 and S7. As depicted in Fig. S6, it was clear that the sorption processes were rapid. Within 80 min, the N,S-CDs-LDH achieved ∼ 99% removal rates and Kd values of >104 mL g−1 for the metal ions. The fitting curves are shown in Fig. 5 and the calculated kinetic constants for the pseudo-first and pseudo-second order models are listed in Table 7 . According to the correlation of determination, R2, the pseudo-second-order model can be accepted as the kinetic mechanism for Hg2+ and Ag+ adsorption on N,S-CDs-LDH and confirms that the ions have been adsorbed via chemical reactions [57]. This observation is consistent with the fact that sulfur groups on the surface of the N,S-CDs can adsorb the Hg2+ and Ag+ via complexation reaction.

The desorption of Hg2+ and Ag+ were performed by using o.1 mol L−1 of EDTA. Obtained results showed that the desorption efficiencies Hg2+ and Ag+ were 98.01 and 99.23% after six recycles, respectively. The good reproducibility suggested that N,S-CDs-LDH could be used as economic and recycle adsorbent for metal-contaminated wastewater treatment.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from Tarbiat Modares University References.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.05.055.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
